critique.summary: The article provides a detailed overview of the new features, preview updates, and deprecations introduced in Java 26. It covers various JEPs (JDK Enhancement Proposals) including improvements in garbage collection, HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, the Vector API, and pattern matching enhancements, among others.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new features such as ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector (JEP 516) and improvements to the G1 garbage collector to reduce synchronization overhead (JEP 522).
The HTTP Client API now supports HTTP/3 (JEP 517), allowing faster and more reliable web communications with fallback mechanisms to HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1.
Structured concurrency (JEP 525) is previewed again, providing a hierarchical and scoped approach to concurrency that improves error handling and cancellation.
Lazy constants (JEP 526) offer a new way to defer initialization of immutable objects safely and efficiently, improving startup performance and concurrency safety.
The Vector API (JEP 529) continues incubation, enabling high-performance vector computations using SIMD instructions.
Pattern matching for primitive types in instanceof and switch statements is previewed again (JEP 530), enhancing type safety and expressiveness.
Java 26 prepares to restrict mutation of final fields via reflection (JEP 500), aiming to improve program correctness and JVM optimizations.
Several features remain in preview status, requiring the --enable-preview flag to use.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately summarizes the official JEPs and features introduced in Java 26 as documented by the OpenJDK project and other authoritative Java sources. The descriptions of the new garbage collection improvements, HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, Vector API, and pattern matching align with the official JEP documentation. The explanations about deprecations and future plans for final field mutation restrictions also correspond with the JEP 500 proposal. The article provides technical details and code examples consistent with publicly available information, indicating a high level of accuracy.
considerations: The article does not mention potential compatibility issues or migration challenges developers might face when adopting Java 26 features, especially those in preview.
It does not discuss the performance implications or benchmarks of the new features beyond general expectations.
The article assumes familiarity with Java internals and JEP terminology, which might limit accessibility for less experienced readers.
Security implications of new features, such as HTTP/3 support or cryptographic PEM encoding, are not explored in depth.
The article does not address the timeline or roadmap for when preview features might become stable or mandatory.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion reflects a mix of appreciation for Java's evolution and criticism of its culture, legacy issues, and ecosystem. Several commenters highlight Java's modernization and new features, while others express frustration with its complexity, legacy baggage, and Android's lag in adopting updates.

comments.author: ludovicianul
comments.text: I program in Java for more than 15 years now. I can resonate with people hating the language from it's early days due to the experience with all the enterprisy features and over abstractions. Or confunding Java with the Spring ecosystem. But Java came a long way over the years. It's now what many would call a "modern" language. It's less verbose, has many of the features people find appealing in Scala and Kotlin and it can even compile to native binaries using GraalVM. This made building CLIs in Java feasible. Or lambdas.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and informed perspective on Java's evolution, highlighting concrete improvements and features that align with known developments.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: Accurately describes recent Java features and their implications, showing good understanding of language design and concurrency improvements.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: Correctly notes the removal of outdated and problematic components, reflecting common developer sentiment about Java applets.

comments.author: einrealist
comments.text: I have been using Java since version 1.4. Both the language and its ecosystem have come a long way since then. I endured the height of the EJB phase. I adopted Spring when version 1.2 was released. I spent hours fighting with IDEs to run OSGi bundles. I hated building UIs with Swing/AWT, many of which are still in use today and are gradually being replaced by lovely JavaFX. When I look at code I wrote around 12 years ago, I'm amazed at how much I've matured too.
comments.analysis: Provides a credible historical overview of Java's ecosystem and personal growth, consistent with known Java history.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about Java's community culture possibly hindering adoption, though the comparison to Go is subjective and less precise.

comments.author: nameconflicts
comments.text: I think Java became popular because of Sun. My experience with Java-based apps is bad. Laggy, resource-heavy, IDE bound, and it causes premature hardware performance degradation.
comments.analysis: Expresses common criticisms about Java applications, though the claim about hardware degradation is exaggerated and not well supported.

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: As someone who has been out of the Java world for many years, but recently forced back into it due to Android dev requirements, Post lawsuit, what is the relationship between Android (Google) and Java now?  When can we expect 26 on Android?  On that note, why is Android always so far behind?  Is it because Kotlin is primary or is it deeper?  Did the lawsuit play a role?
comments.analysis: Asks relevant questions about Android's lag in Java version adoption, reflecting common concerns but does not provide answers.

comments.author: larrylawnmower
comments.text: Are people still using gigantic xml file abominations (like pom.xml) with java? No thanks. Web moved years ago, and now most LOB software appear to also have switched to languages that are not that heavy weight and riddled with software patterns. And then the sdk still require accepting an EULA. Gtfo.
comments.analysis: Critiques legacy build tools and XML configurations, which is a valid point though somewhat dismissive of Java's ongoing ecosystem improvements.

comments.author: rusakov-field
comments.text: Ah , Java, a language I pray I never have to ever touch again.
comments.analysis: A purely emotional comment expressing dislike without substantive reasoning or constructive input.

comments.author: GTP
comments.text: I haven't read a Java manual since the time of Java 8. Do you have any books or other resources you could recommend to catch up with all that has changed in these years?
comments.analysis: Seeks updated learning resources, showing engagement but does not contribute analysis or factual content.
